---
title: "Discussion and Conclusion"
author: "Yiming Cao, Zichen Fan, Xinyin Miao, Teresa Sha"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
---

# Discussion

## Quasi-Poisson Time Series GLM (Yiming Cao)

### Key results

Using a quasi-Poisson GLM on daily city-wide EMS counts for 2024, I estimated a rate ratio of 0.974 (95% CI 0.932–1.017, p = 0.228) per 10 μg/m³ increase in same-day PM2.5 for mental health-related calls, and 1.089 (95% CI 0.837–1.416, p = 0.526) for suicide-related calls. Neither association reached statistical significance. The dispersion parameter was 2.24 for mental health and 0.95 for suicide, which is why the quasi-Poisson variance adjustment mattered for the mental health model but less so for suicide. The PM2.5 estimate for mental health was stable across time-trend df from 3 to 6 (RR between 0.972 and 0.976), but dropping the top 5% of PM2.5 days moved the estimate from 0.974 to 1.015. The weekend indicator was the strongest predictor in the mental health model (fewer calls on weekends, p < 0.001), consistent with what we saw in the EDA.

### Interpretation

I didn't find a clear short-term city-wide association between PM2.5 and either outcome in 2024. A few things likely contribute to this. First, 2024 was a relatively clean air year in NYC: the 95th percentile of daily PM2.5 was only 12.9 μg/m³, well below the levels seen in studies that found positive associations. With limited exposure contrast, the model has little to work with at the upper end of the distribution. Second, the sensitivity analysis that excluded high-PM2.5 days changed the point estimate from slightly below 1 to slightly above 1, which suggests the main estimate is sensitive to a handful of high-exposure days and should not be over-interpreted either direction. Third, EMS calls are a coarse outcome. Not every psychiatric crisis triggers a 911 call, and the reverse is also true, so we may be missing the cases that are more responsive to environmental stressors.

When I put these numbers next to the other methods in our project, the picture is reasonably consistent. The case-crossover results from Teresa also had IRRs close to 1 with CIs crossing the null, and Xinyin's nonlinear specification did not reveal a strong exposure-response shape either. The one place where something different shows up is Zichen's borough-level mixed model, which found a borderline-significant 3.6% decrease in mental health calls per 10 μg/m³ (RR = 0.964, p = 0.033). That discrepancy probably reflects the added spatial variation the mixed model uses; borough-specific baselines absorb part of the confounding that a city-wide GLM cannot. With only four boroughs it is hard to lean on that result too hard.

These findings fit the mixed literature on short-term PM2.5 and psychiatric outcomes. Kim et al. (2010) reported a 10.1% increase in suicide risk per IQR increase in PM2.5 (1 day prior) in seven South Korean cities, where ambient PM2.5 is typically much higher than in NYC. Bakian et al. (2015) found a smaller effect for PM2.5 (about 5% higher odds of suicide for exposure 2–3 days before death) in Salt Lake County, with the stronger signal coming from NO2. On the mental health side, Bernardini et al. (2020) examined psychiatric emergency admissions in two Italian hospitals and found no association with PM2.5; only ozone was significant. Our 2024 NYC result lines up with the idea that short-term PM2.5 signals show up more clearly where PM2.5 is higher. In cities near the WHO guideline, other pollutants or multi-day lags may matter more than same-day PM2.5.

### References

- Bakian AV, Huber RS, Coon H, Gray D, Wilson P, McMahon WM, Renshaw PF. Acute air pollution exposure and risk of suicide completion. *Am J Epidemiol.* 2015;181(5):295–303.
- Kim C, Jung SH, Kang DR, Kim HC, Moon KT, Hur NW, Shin DC, Suh I. Ambient particulate matter as a risk factor for suicide. *Am J Psychiatry.* 2010;167(9):1100–1107.
- Bernardini F, Attademo L, Trezzi R, et al. Air pollutants and daily number of admissions to psychiatric emergency services: evidence for detrimental mental health effects of ozone. *Epidemiol Psychiatr Sci.* 2020;29:e66.

---

## Nonlinear Exposure-Response Analysis (Xinyin Miao)

### Key results

I fit quasi-Poisson nonlinear exposure–response models using a natural spline for same-day PM2.5 (3 df), adjusting for seasonal trend, temperature, weekend, and precipitation, and compared them with the corresponding linear specifications for both outcomes.

For mental health EMS calls, the nonlinear curve was approximately flat across the observed PM2.5 range with no clear threshold or positive trend, and the F-test did not indicate a significant departure from the linear model. For suicide-related EMS calls, the curve showed a slight dip at lower concentrations and a gradual rise at higher concentrations, but confidence intervals were wide throughout, reflecting the high variability of this low-count outcome. Sensitivity analyses confirmed that the mental health curve was robust to alternative PM2.5 spline df, time-trend specification, and precipitation functional form; the one notable finding was that excluding days above the 95th percentile PM2.5 (~12.9 μg/m³) attenuated the high-end pattern, suggesting modest leverage from a small number of extreme-exposure days.

### Interpretation

The absence of a strong or clearly nonlinear exposure–response pattern for same-day PM2.5 in 2024 NYC is consistent with limited exposure contrast in this dataset. The observed PM2.5 range was narrow — the 95th percentile was only about 12.9 μg/m³, well below WHO 24-hour guidelines and well below the concentrations studied in most published research reporting nonlinear associations. When exposure varies little and stays near the lower tail of the distribution seen in prior work, even a true nonlinear relationship would be difficult to detect: the curve is estimated primarily in a region where, under most proposed biological mechanisms, effects are expected to be small.

The sensitivity analysis excluding the top 5% of PM2.5 days is informative in this respect. The few highest-exposure days appear to anchor the right end of the curve, and their exclusion shifts the curve modestly, suggesting that the estimated nonlinear shape at high PM2.5 is driven by a very small number of observations. This echoes the finding from Yiming's linear GLM, where the same exclusion moved the point estimate from 0.974 to 1.015. Together, these results caution against interpreting the nonlinear curve at the upper tail as a reliable signal.

More broadly, the null short-term finding is informative when read alongside the broader literature. Yang et al. (2023) used UK Biobank data (n = 389,185; median PM2.5 ~9.9 μg/m³, comparable to 2024 NYC) and found that long-term multi-pollutant exposure was associated with increased depression and anxiety risk, with nonlinear exposure–response curves that were steeper at lower concentrations and plateaued at higher exposures. The contrast between their long-term findings and our null short-term result suggests that the accumulation of PM2.5 exposure over months or years, rather than a single day's exposure, may be the more relevant pathway for psychiatric outcomes — particularly in low-concentration settings like NYC where acute spikes are rare and the daily range of variation is narrow. Studies that found short-term PM2.5 associations, such as Kim et al. (2010) in South Korea and Bakian et al. (2015) in Salt Lake County, were conducted in settings with substantially higher or more variable ambient concentrations, providing greater statistical power to detect acute effects. This suggests that whether a nonlinear short-term exposure–response relationship can be detected may depend critically on exposure contrast, and that data from a single clean-air year in NYC are likely insufficient to characterize the acute curve at the upper tail reliably.

### References

- Bakian AV, Huber RS, Coon H, Gray D, Wilson P, McMahon WM, Renshaw PF. Acute air pollution exposure and risk of suicide completion. *Am J Epidemiol.* 2015;181(5):295–303.
- Kim C, Jung SH, Kang DR, et al. Ambient particulate matter as a risk factor for suicide. *Am J Psychiatry.* 2010;167(9):1100–1107.
- Yang T, Wang J, Huang J, Kelly FJ, Li G. Long-term exposure to multiple ambient air pollutants and association with incident depression and anxiety. *JAMA Psychiatry.* 2023;80(4):305–313.

---

## Borough-Level Mixed Effects Analysis (Zichen Fan)

### Key results

The primary random-intercept negative binomial mixed model found a statistically significant negative association between scaled PM2.5 and mental health EMS calls (RR = 0.987 per ~3.7 µg/m³ increase; 95% CI: 0.974–0.999; p = 0.033). An ICC of 0.525 for mental health call volume confirmed that over half the total outcome variance was attributable to between-borough differences. The RI standard deviation (σ^0=0.133) reflected meaningful baseline heterogeneity across boroughs, with Brooklyn and Manhattan showing above-average baseline call volumes and Queens the lowest. The LRT comparing the RI and RIRS models was non-significant (χ2=0.882, df = 2, p = 0.643), therefore no significant improvement in model fit with RS added. Furthermore, the RS model exhibited singular convergence, likely due to the limited number of spatial clusters (n=4), justifying the selection of the more parsimonious RI model.

### Interpretation

The negative direction is puzzling — but consistent across all four analyses. This is worth emphasizing. The city-wide quasi-Poisson model gave RR = 0.974 (*p* = 0.228) for mental health; Case-crossover returned IRRs hovering just below 1.0 across all lag structures; nonlinear curve showed a gradual downward slope at higher PM2.5 concentrations with a modest mid-range hump. None of these pointed toward a positive association for mental health calls. This model is the only one to achieve statistical significance is likely a power story, the panel structure (480 observations, repeated within borough) gives more leverage than the city-level 124-day series, not because the effect is fundamentally different but because clustering is now explicitly modeled rather than ignored.

Why might higher PM2.5 days be associated with fewer mental health EMS calls? A few potential explanations could be discussed: 1. On visibly polluted days people may stay indoors, reducing exposure to the social environments (transit, crowds, street-level interactions) that often precede acute psychiatric crises. 2. In NYC, the highest-PM2.5 days are often wildfire smoke intrusion events rather than locally-generated traffic pollution. These events may not carry the same constellation of co-stressors (noise, heat, crowding) that drive EMS calls on typical high-pollution days.

ICC results suggest that random-intercept approach is more appropriate here than a fixed-intercept model, as it borrows strength across boroughs while still controlling for unmeasured time-invariant confounders. The baseline call volume gradient (Brooklyn > Manhattan > Bronx > Queens) is consistent with known disparities in mental health burden and EMS utilization across NYC boroughs, likely reflecting socioeconomic vulnerability, housing density, and differential access to outpatient psychiatric services.

Using only K=4 boroughs, the math allow the model has low degrees of freedom to calculate how much the slopes actually vary between those four groups. So, the non-significant LRT should be interpreted cautiously.Cannot rule out meaningful effect modification by borough, particularly given that borough-specific total slopes from the RIRS model show a visible gradient (Brooklyn and Manhattan appearing more negative than Bronx and Queens), which could reflect differences in population vulnerability, green space, or co-exposure patterns.

---

## Case-crossover Analysis (Teresa Sha)

### Key results

In this study, we examined the short-term relationship between PM2.5 and EMS calls in New York City. Overall, PM2.5 shows a weak association with mental health–related EMS calls. From both the plots and the models, the relationship does not appear to be clearly linear. There is a slight increase at lower to moderate PM2.5 levels, but the curve becomes flatter at higher levels. For suicide-related calls, the association is even weaker and less consistent. In contrast, temperature shows a clearer positive relationship with both outcomes. We also observed strong temporal patterns, including seasonality and a noticeable decrease in EMS calls on weekends.

### Interpretation

These results suggest that short-term PM2.5 exposure may not be a strong trigger for acute mental health–related EMS events in this dataset. One possible explanation is that PM2.5 levels during the study period are relatively low most of the time, so the effect is not very obvious. Another explanation is that mental health outcomes are influenced by many factors, such as social and psychological stress, which may be more important than short-term environmental exposure. The non-linear pattern we observed could also be due to limited data at higher PM2.5 levels, making the curve less stable in that range. On the other hand, the stronger association with temperature suggests that weather conditions may play a more important role in short-term variation in EMS calls.

When comparing our findings with previous studies, the results are generally consistent with the mixed evidence reported in the literature. Some studies have found positive associations between PM2.5 and mental health outcomes, such as increased psychiatric visits or depressive symptoms. However, other studies report weak or inconsistent short-term effects, especially when focusing on acute outcomes. Our findings are closer to those studies showing limited short-term impact, which may be related to differences in exposure levels, outcome definitions, or study design. In our case, EMS calls may capture acute crises rather than underlying mental health conditions, which could also explain the weaker association.

This study has several strengths. We used daily EMS data, which reflect real-time health demand, and combined multiple data sources, including air pollution and weather data. We also applied both a time-series model as the main analysis and a case-crossover design as a sensitivity analysis. In addition, we considered non-linear exposure-response relationships, lag effects, and potential effect modification. At the same time, there are some limitations. PM2.5 data are not available for every day, which reduces the sample size and statistical power. Exposure is based on city-level averages and may not represent individual exposure. EMS data may not capture all mental health events or their severity. The case-crossover analysis is applied to aggregated daily data rather than individual-level data, and there may still be some residual confounding from unmeasured factors.

From a public health perspective, our results suggest that PM2.5 may not be the main short-term factor influencing mental health–related EMS demand. Instead, temperature and time-related patterns appear to be more important. This indicates that interventions focusing on extreme weather conditions and temporal patterns may be more effective for managing short-term EMS demand related to mental health.

### References

*(to be filled in)*

---

# Conclusion

*(to be drafted together — suggested structure below)*

### Research gap

*(brief reminder of the gap our project addresses)*

### Strengths

*(e.g., multiple complementary methods on the same 2024 NYC data, spatial + temporal angles, sensitivity analyses)*

### Limitations

*(e.g., single-year data, ecological design, limited exposure contrast, EMS as a coarse outcome proxy, only 4 boroughs with PM2.5 monitors)*

### Future applications

*(e.g., multi-year analysis, adding other pollutants, finer spatial resolution, integrating socioeconomic modifiers)*

---

# Team Contributions

| Member | Method | Past contributions | This check-in | Future responsibilities |
|--------|--------|--------------------|---------------|-------------------------|
| Yiming Cao | Quasi-Poisson Time Series GLM | Data acquisition, EDA and figures; methods proposal section for GLM; preliminary GLM results; borough-level dataset preprocessing (`prep_borough.py`) for mixed model | Discussion section for Quasi-Poisson GLM; literature review; set up shared Rmd for this check-in | Final write-up of GLM section; contribute to conclusion |
| Zichen Fan | Borough-Level Mixed Effects | Methods proposal section for mixed model; preliminary mixed-effects model fitting, plots, and key results; formatting updates | *(to be filled in)* | *(to be filled in)* |
| Xinyin Miao | Nonlinear Exposure-Response | Introduction of project; repository initial setup; methods proposal section for nonlinear analysis; preliminary nonlinear analysis and sensitivity (nonlinear precipitation); DAG |Discussion section for nonlinear analysis; literature review | Final write-up of nonlinear section; contribute to conclusion |
| Teresa Sha | Case-crossover | Initial case-crossover Rmd; case-crossover sensitivity analysis; preliminary case-crossover results | *(to be filled in)* | *(to be filled in)* |
